Good writing piece. Pointing out the ironies between the two politicians from opposing dynasties was a good wake up call for their respective followers. This event really did put a spotlight between political courage and political showmanship.

It only strengthens my opinion that i oppose the “Marcos resign” calls. The president needs to finish his term until 2028. We all know who will be next in line if ever PBBM heeds the call. 

Will the Filipinos benefit if the president heeds the call? Will the VP taking over improve the lives of Filipinos? Will the current hot issue on corruption be resolved once she sits as president? Will the senators, lawmakers and other government officials, especially those known to be dds be held accountable for the flood control corruption schemes? 

Will it benefit the Filipinos if the foreign policy directions pivot back from USA back to China? Do we really benefit becoming a province of China? 

Personally speaking, i would have preferred former VP Leni Robredo to have won the 2022 elections and became president. But Uniteam won. But for the current political noise, I still prefer PBBM to finish his term until 2028 instead of Sara taking over just for those concerns i mentioned. Marcos is the lesser evil between the two. This post further proves Marcos brand is better than the Duterte brand. 

Nevertheless, netizens who follow this page probably share the view that we need to do away with political dynasties and a law abolishing these political dynasties should be passed and implemented. 

Political dynasties have been proven to be the source of corruption. So these dynasties need to be curbed if not totally eradicated.

CONFIDENTIAL FUNDS: What the Law Actually Requires

Here is the single most important truth:

YES, confidential operations protect operational details
(identities of operatives, targets, operations, locations)

BUT...
NO, they cannot use fake names, joke names, invented identities, or unverifiable persons in their liquidation documents.

That is explicitly prohibited by COA rules, DBM rules, and the Joint Circular governing confidential funds.

Let’s break it down in plain Filipino + English:

📌 1. Confidential funds still require REAL documentation.

The governing rule is:

COA–DBM–DILG Joint Circular 2015-01
+
COA Circular 2021-004 (updated rules on cash advances and liquidation)

These require:

Minimum required documentation:

✔ Real, identifiable payees
✔ Valid acknowledgment receipts
✔ Specific expense categories
✔ Purpose aligned with intelligence/confidential mandates
✔ Proof that the activity happened (reports, summaries—classified if needed)
✔ Certification from the head of agency affirming authenticity

The law never allows: 
Fake names
Fictional persons
Joke names
Blank or unreadable signatures
"Non-existent individuals”
800 signatures without names
1,322 names with no PSA records

That is not confidentiality.

That is falsification under:

RA 3019 (Anti-Graft)
RA 6713 (Code of Conduct)
Art. 171 & 172 of the RPC (Falsification)

COA rules on fraudulent documents

📌 2. Confidentiality covers content... NOT the existence of real payees.

The law allows you to hide:

the target
the operation
the methods
the intel gathered

But you cannot hide:

the fact that public funds were spent
on real human beings
who actually performed real tasks
with real receipts
Confidentiality ≠ anonymity.

COA itself has stated in multiple decisions:

“Classified operations may justify redacted documentation,
but not the use of fictitious names or unverifiable persons.”

Imagine mo, yung taong nagsasabing “ipaglalaban ko ang mga manggagawa” tapos siya pala may car collection na pwede nang maging museum at real-estate portfolio na mas marami pa sa branches ng Jollibee sa SoCal.

Ang sakit sa puso kapag naisip mo na ang party-list system natin – na dapat talaga voice ng marginalized sectors – ay na-hijack na naman ng mga oligarchs at contractors. Classic “cong-tractor” move: pumasok ka bilang “representative ng masa,” tapos habang nakaupo ka sa Kongreso at nagdedeliberate ng budget, yung pamilya mo naman pala ang unang-unang nakakaalam kung saan pupunta ang bilyon-bilyong pondo ng DPWH. Pre-ordered pa raw ang projects – parang Shopee lang, add to cart agad bago pa ma-approve ang GAA! “Kailangan ko ‘yan for my constituents… and also for my pocket, char!”

At yung flood control anomaly? Perfect example ng irony: yung taong dapat nagpo-protekta sa mga tao laban sa baha, siya pa pala ang dahilan kung bakit patuloy na lumulubog ang Pilipinas – figuratively and literally.

Sa totoo lang, hindi na ako magugulat kung bukas may makita tayong bagong vlog title: “A Day in the Life of a Workers’ Rep: Morning hearing sa House, lunch sa yacht, afternoon site visit… sa California.” 😂

Pero beyond the chismis at meme potential, this case is depressing kasi pinapakita niya kung gaano ka-broken ang checks and balances natin. Kahit may COA red flags, tax evasion cases, defective projects, at literal plunder complaints, nakaka-upo pa rin at nakaka-jackpot pa rin ng bilyones. Kung ganito ang “representation” ng construction workers, baka mas maganda pang i-represent na lang sila ni Bob the Builder – at least siya honest na “Can we fix it? Yes we can!” hindi “Can we fix the bidding? Yes we can!”

Hay naku, mga kababayan, pagdating sa Philippine politics, talagang world-class ang level ng creativity pagdating sa “legal loopholes” at “separate juridical personality.” Basahin mo ‘tong buong post na ‘to, parang season finale ng pinakamalupit na teleserye: may betrayal, may conflict of interest, may ROI na mas malaki pa sa crypto bull run, at syempre, may “cleared” na ending kahit halatang may something fishy. Pero seryoso, sobrang daming layers ‘to, kaya pag-usapan natin ng matino pero may konting tawa, kasi kung hindi, baka umiyak na lang tayo sa sistema.

Una, yung core defense ng COMELEC: “Separate juridical personality daw po.” Meaning, kahit 99.331% pala ni Lawrence Lubiano ang Centerways Construction, at siya rin ang president, iba pa rin daw ang tao sa korporasyon. So pwede raw siya mag-donate ng ₱30M as “personal capacity” kahit yung pera halos siguradong galing sa kita ng kumpanya na umaani ng bilyones sa DPWH flood control projects — karamihan pa sa Sorsogon, home turf ni Chiz. 

Bro, sa totoo lang, parang sinabi ng korte: “Okay lang mag-donate ang may-ari ng Mercury Drug kahit banned ang Mercury Drug mag-donate, kasi ‘personal’ daw yung pera niya.” Eh di wow, maganda ‘yan! Next election, lahat na lang ng contractor magwi-withdraw ng “personal funds” from their company accounts tapos donate. Solved na ang funding problems ng lahat ng kandidato! Certified legal hack ‘to, mga pare.

Tapos yung conflict of interest ni COMELEC Chair George Garcia? Grabe, parang script sa pelikula ni Erik Matti. 
- Election lawyer ni Chiz noong 2022 → naging COMELEC Chair after 6 weeks. 
- Pumirma pa ng COC at malamang tumulong sa SOCE kung saan nakalagay yung ₱30M donation. 
- Tapos nung may kaso na, nag-inhibit siya… pero yung buong resolution galing pa rin sa department under him. 

Parang judge na dating abogado ng accused tapos sabi niya “Sige, hindi ako magde-decide, pero yung staff ko na lang.” Eh syempre, alam na ng staff kung sino boss nila. Tapos yung misis pa ni Garcia, naging Deputy Secretary sa Senate nung Senate President si Chiz. Ay sus, parang incestuous na ang relationship ng executive, legislative, at COMELEC. Sana all may ganyang network, di ba?

Pero yung pinaka-malupit talaga: yung ROI ng mga contractors. 
- ₱30M donation → ₱16.67B contracts after (Centerways) → 555x return in 3 years. 
- ₱20M kay BBM → bilyones din sa Rudhil Construction. 
- ₱1M lang kay Quirante → ₱3.8B agad. 

Mga bes, sa stock market nga hindi mo makikita yang ganitong return! Parang nag-invest sila sa “Philippine Politics High-Yield Fund” na guaranteed 500–3000% in 2–3 years. Samantalang tayo, nagti-tipid sa kape para makapag-invest sa mutual fund na 8% per year pa lang, natutuwa na. Unfair talaga ang mundo.

At syempre, yung “precedent” na ginawa ng COMELEC clearing Chiz — automatic shield na ‘to kay President Marcos, kay VP Sara, kay Villanueva, Gatchalian, Zubiri, etc. Kasi parehong-pareho lang ang pattern: personal donation daw from the owner, hindi from the company. Kung guilty si Chiz, guilty din lahat sila. Kaya ayun, “not guilty” lahat para safe. Classic Philippine politics: kapag maraming malalaking isda ang maaapektuhan, biglang nagiging “very strict interpretation” ng batas para lang makalusot lahat. Unity in impunity, ganern.

Tapos yung ₱35M “missing” sa retained earnings ng Centerways na biglang nawala nung nag-restate ng financial statements? Tapos exact ₱30M yung donation? Ay grabe, parang magic trick ni David Blaine. “Now you see it, now you don’t.” Tapos walang imbestigasyon? BIR na lang daw magche-check. Eh kung BIR din naman controlled ng executive… alam na.

Sa madaling salita: 
Legal man ang clearing kay Chiz on technical grounds, pero sa mata ng common sense at moral compass ng ordinaryong Pilipino — sobrang bulok. This is not just about Chiz. This is about an entire system na ginagamit na ATM ng iilang contractors at politicians. Yung flood control funds na supposed to protect us from baha, ginagamit pala para magpapakain sa political machinery. Tapos kapag may baha pa rin, magugulat tayo kung bakit ganun. Eh kasi yung budget, nasa bulsa na ng mga “donors” at “donees.”

Pero may silver lining: kahit cleared si Chiz sa COMELEC, natanggal siya as Senate President at may pending ethics case pa rin + Ombudsman probe. At least may konting karma agad. At yung mga rally sa Luneta at EDSA — sign na gising na ang tao. Sana tuluy-tuloy na.

Final verdict ko: 
Yung decision ng COMELEC ay technically defensible, pero morally bankrupt. Parang sinabi nila, “Oo nga bawal mag-donate ang contractor, pero kung ikaw ang contractor, pwede.” Classic Philippine exception talaga: may batas, pero may “unless you’re powerful” clause.

Kaya mga kababayan, next election, tandaan natin ‘to. Kasi kung hindi, forever na tayong baha — literal at figurative.

LET'S TAKE A LOOK AT THE SEQUENCE OF DATES (see attached files):
1) August 22, 2022 – the date of VP Sara’s request letter to Dept. of Budget and Management (DBM). 
2) September 15, 2022- DBM replied to OVP to ask for further supporting documents of its request.
3) September 19, 2022- OVP complied and submitted to DBM the requested supporting documents.
4) October 18, 2022- DBM submitted to the Office of the Executive Secretary (ES Bersamin) a Memorandum for the President, recommending the grant of P125 million only (half of the requested amount) kasi 2nd semester of the year nalang naman natitira. In the same Memorandum, DBM identified that the CONTINGENT FUND as the source of the OVP request.
5) November 28, 2022– ES Bersamin released a Memorandum granting the release of the P125 million from the CONTINGENT FUND with corresponding compliance according to the law.
6) December 13, 2022– DBM issued Special Allotment Release Order (SARO) to the OVP the requested P125 million Confidential Fund to be sourced from CONTINGENT FUND.
7) December 15, 2022- DBM issued and released the Notice of Cash Allocation (NCA) to OVP.
8) December 21, 2022- the start of actual payment of OVP’s CF obligation
9) December 31, 2022- end of Fiscal Year 2022
